whatTheRoleDoes: Sales roles turn product value into revenue by prospecting, running discovery, handling objections, and moving deals through a buying process.
strengthsToEmphasize: Persuasive communication and comfort initiating conversations
Ownership of outcomes instead of just task completion
Energy in relationship building and commercial momentum
storiesToTell: A time you influenced a decision or got buy-in for a recommendation
A situation where you handled resistance and still moved things forward
An example of owning a target, campaign, or growth-oriented outcome
biggestGapsToClose: Proof that you can handle direct customer or prospect conversations
Evidence that you enjoy performance pressure and consistent outreach
Clear examples of asking for commitment or next steps
keyActions: Practice discovery and objection-handling scripts with peers
Build quantified stories around wins, targets, or growth outcomes
Learn common SaaS sales metrics and funnel terminology

whatTheRoleDoes: Sales Engineers support the sales cycle by translating technical capabilities, running demos, answering system questions, and connecting product functionality to business value.
strengthsToEmphasize: Technical curiosity paired with customer-facing composure
Ability to explain systems clearly to mixed audiences
Comfort switching between product detail and business outcomes
storiesToTell: A time you learned a tool deeply enough to teach or demo it
A situation where you translated technical detail for a nontechnical audience
A moment where troubleshooting or systems thinking changed the outcome
biggestGapsToClose: Live-demo credibility and technical fluency
Evidence that you can answer technical questions under pressure
Examples of customer-facing technical problem solving
keyActions: Practice demoing a real product or sandbox workflow out loud
Build familiarity with APIs, integrations, and solution design basics
Create stories that tie technical detail to customer value

whatTheRoleDoes: Project and Program Management roles coordinate timelines, dependencies, risks, and communication so cross-functional work actually lands. TPM-leaning work adds more technical dependency management.
strengthsToEmphasize: Building structure, timelines, and accountability across teams
Reliable follow-through and visible execution discipline
Calm coordination when many moving parts need alignment
storiesToTell: A time you kept a complex initiative on track across multiple people
A situation where you improved a process or operating cadence
An example of surfacing risk early and preventing downstream issues
biggestGapsToClose: Evidence that you can lead through influence instead of authority
Examples of risk management, prioritization, and stakeholder updates
For TPM-leaning paths, stronger systems and technical fluency
keyActions: Run a project tracker for a club, internship, or volunteer initiative
Practice concise written status updates with risks, owners, and next steps
For technical variants, learn how dependencies, tickets, and release workflows operate

whatTheRoleDoes: Product Management blends customer insight, analytics, prioritization, and stakeholder alignment to shape what gets built and why.
strengthsToEmphasize: Customer insight translated into clear tradeoff-aware recommendations
Analytical framing under ambiguity
Ownership of decisions and cross-functional influence
storiesToTell: A time you discovered a real user need and reframed the problem
A situation where you weighed competing priorities and made a recommendation
An example of aligning different stakeholders around a decision
biggestGapsToClose: Real evidence of customer discovery, prioritization, and tradeoff judgment
Proof that you can handle ambiguity without defaulting to vague ideas
Examples of owning an outcome instead of just contributing analysis
keyActions: Do structured user interviews and summarize patterns in a short brief
Create a sample prioritization memo with tradeoffs and assumptions
Build one project where you define the problem, coordinate execution, and reflect on the outcome

whatTheRoleDoes: Finance roles support planning and business decisions through forecasting, variance analysis, operating metrics, and scenario modeling.
strengthsToEmphasize: Quantitative discipline and comfort with business metrics
Analytical reasoning that leads to actionable recommendations
Reliable communication of numbers and assumptions
storiesToTell: A time you built or improved an analysis that informed a decision
A situation where you found the real driver behind a metric shift
An example of balancing speed with accuracy in a numerical task
biggestGapsToClose: Hands-on proof with models, forecasts, or KPI analysis
Fluency with finance vocabulary and business cases
Confidence explaining assumptions behind the numbers
keyActions: Practice forecasting, scenario analysis, and budget-style modeling
Create a portfolio example that explains a business metric problem clearly
Learn SaaS metrics like ARR, churn, CAC, and gross margin

whatTheRoleDoes: Accounting roles maintain financial accuracy through reconciliations, controls, close processes, and dependable records that the business can trust.
strengthsToEmphasize: Accuracy, reliability, and strong control-oriented habits
Consistency with recurring workflows and documentation
Quantitative confidence paired with disciplined follow-through
storiesToTell: A time your accuracy or review process prevented an error
A situation where you handled detailed work under deadline pressure
An example of improving a repetitive process without sacrificing control
biggestGapsToClose: Proof of comfort with precision-heavy numerical work
Examples of control thinking and disciplined documentation
Signals that you can handle recurring deadlines reliably
keyActions: Practice reconciliations, statement logic, and control-oriented reviews
Build examples that show precision and repeatability under deadlines
Learn how month-end close and revenue recognition basics work

whatTheRoleDoes: Customer Success helps customers adopt a product, navigate issues, and realize value over time so relationships stay healthy and renewals stay strong.
strengthsToEmphasize: Customer empathy without losing structure or accountability
Clear communication in moments of friction or uncertainty
Cross-functional follow-through that protects the customer relationship
storiesToTell: A time you calmed a frustrated person and moved toward a solution
A situation where you coordinated internally to deliver for an external stakeholder
An example of building trust over time, not just solving a single issue
biggestGapsToClose: Evidence that you can manage relationships while still driving action
Examples of customer-facing follow-through and adoption thinking
Proof that you can balance empathy with commercial or operational judgment
keyActions: Practice discovery and check-in conversations with a service mindset
Learn onboarding, adoption, churn, and renewal language used in SaaS
Build stories that combine trust-building with coordinated problem solving

whatTheRoleDoes: Business Systems Analysts improve workflows by gathering requirements, documenting needs, translating across teams, and making systems easier to use.
strengthsToEmphasize: Systems thinking grounded in business process reality
Analytical problem solving with technical curiosity
Translation across operational, technical, and stakeholder contexts
storiesToTell: A time you diagnosed a workflow issue and clarified the real requirement
A situation where you improved how data or tools moved through a process
An example of bridging business language and system logic
biggestGapsToClose: Evidence of hands-on requirements or configuration work
Proof that you can connect system behavior to business outcomes
Stronger examples of process improvement with tooling involved
keyActions: Practice writing requirements, swimlanes, and before-after workflow notes
Learn basic system concepts like fields, objects, integrations, and permissions
Use a sandbox or low-code tool to document and improve a simple process
